walkthrough.title: Place the Alderley Hall Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Obsession set up. First, find the Alderley Hall board. This is the central game board that tracks rounds and holds card decks. Place it on the table with the 16-round side face up for standard play, or the 20-round side for extended play. For your first game, go with the standard 16-round side.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Round Track and Card Decks
walkthrough.text: Place the white pawn on the first space of the round track on the Alderley Hall board. Now shuffle the 30 victory point cards and place them face down on their designated board space. Separately shuffle the 10 theme cards and stack them face down on their board space. These theme cards will reveal which tile category matters for each season's courtship.

walkthrough.title: Place Reminder Tokens and Fairchild Cards
walkthrough.text: Place one reminder token per player on the Alderley Hall board, out of the way of other components. These tokens are used when you buy a Monument or Servants' Hall to remind you of their recurring bonuses. Then place the 2 Fairchild guest cards near the board. These are special guests with a crest in a purple frame that winners of courtship rounds will claim during the game.

walkthrough.title: Choose First Player
walkthrough.text: Choose who goes first and give them the purple first-player pawn. Play will proceed clockwise from the first player each round. The first-player pawn passes counterclockwise after each of the first three courtship rounds.

walkthrough.title: Choose Families and Hand Out Components
walkthrough.text: Each player chooses one of the four families. Each family has a unique player board with their family name, a set of 4 reference cards, and their own components. Give each player their family's player board and optionally the organizer strip. The four families each have slightly different starting conditions, so check your reference cards for any special rules.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Start Improvement Tiles
walkthrough.text: Each player receives 5 start improvement tiles: Private Study, Butler's Room, Main Gazebo, Front Parlour, and Bowling Green. Align these tiles under your player board or organizer strip in their proper category columns. These are your starting estate rooms and spaces that you can host activities in right away.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Reputation Wheel
walkthrough.text: Each player gets 4 or 5 reputation counters showing values 1/2, 3/4, 5/6, and Max (plus 7/8 for extended play). Display the number 1 on the center of the reputation wheel at the top right of your player board. Place the rest of the counters aside for later. Then take the black hexagonal prism marker and place it on the 1 space of the reputation wheel. Exception: family Cavendish starts their marker on 4 instead of 1.

walkthrough.title: Take Your Starting Servants
walkthrough.text: Each player receives 5 start servants: a Butler, a Housekeeper, a Valet, a Lady's Maid, and a Footman. Place all of these servants in your Available Service area on your player board. Exception: family York receives 1 additional Footman, for 6 total servants. Also note that family Ponsonby starts with 300 coins.

walkthrough.title: Take Your Family Member Cards
walkthrough.text: Each player takes their 4 family member cards: husband, wife, son, and daughter. These form part of your starting active hand. Exception: family Asquith also receives the dowager countess Maud as a fifth family member. Family members can never be dismissed and do not have prestige requirements to invite.

walkthrough.title: Deal Start Guests
walkthrough.text: Shuffle all 15 start guest cards. Deal 2 start guests to each player. These start guests, combined with your family member cards, form your initial active hand of gentry cards. Start guests have prestige 1 and 0 victory points, and they are identified by a crown symbol below their fleur-de-lis.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Derbyshire Board
walkthrough.text: Place the Derbyshire board on the table. This is the second shared board that holds the Builders' Market, guest supply decks, servant supply, and money. It is the economic hub of the game where you will buy tiles and find new guests.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Guest Decks
walkthrough.text: Take the unused start guests (those not dealt to players) and shuffle them together with the 35 casual guest cards. Stack this combined deck face down on the 1 fleur-de-lis Guests space on the Derbyshire board. Separately, shuffle all 25 prestige guest cards and stack them face down on the 2 fleur-de-lis Guests space. Casual guests have prestige 1-2, while prestige guests have prestige 3-6 and are worth more victory points.

walkthrough.title: Deal Objective Cards
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the 30 objective cards. Deal 5 objective cards to each player for standard play, or 4 for extended play. Place the remaining objective cards face down in their space on the Derbyshire board. Objective cards give you secret scoring goals that award victory points at game end if you complete their conditions.

walkthrough.title: Stock the Servants for Hire
walkthrough.text: On the Servants For Hire space of the Derbyshire board, place servants based on the number of players. Per player, add 1 Valet, 1 Lady's Maid, and 2 Footmen. So for a 3-player game, that is 3 Valets, 3 Lady's Maids, and 6 Footmen. Also place 2 Underbutlers nearby. These Underbutlers are only gained when a player purchases the Butler's Pantry tile.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Money Supply
walkthrough.text: Place the silver coins (100 each) and gold coins (500 each) in the money area of the Derbyshire board. Money is not token-limited in Obsession, so if you run out of physical coins, use substitutes. Remember that family Ponsonby starts with 300 coins from their family ability.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Tile Bag
walkthrough.text: Put all improvement tiles except Monument tiles and unused start tiles into the cloth bag. Then, for 2 or 3 players, add the Sculpture Garden Monument plus 2 or 3 other Monument tiles to the bag. For 4 players, add 5 Monument tiles to the bag. Players may choose which additional Monument tiles to include. If desired, you can exclude the powerful Sculpture Garden or substitute Garden Maze instead.

walkthrough.title: Fill the Builders' Market
walkthrough.text: Draw tiles from the bag to fill the Builders' Market on the Derbyshire board, placing them front side up. Stack any duplicate tiles together, filling one market space per set of identical tiles. Only allow tiles with prestige 1, 2, or 3, plus the Brushing Room, Barn, Butler's Pantry, and Servants' Quarters. Exclude tiles with prestige 4, 5, or 6, Servants' Hall, and all Monuments. Put excluded tiles back in the bag.

walkthrough.title: Sort the Market and Final Check
walkthrough.text: Sort the tiles in the Builders' Market by their tile sorting numbers, ascending from left to right. The sorting number is the small black number at the bottom right of each tile. If all players agree the market looks bizarre, such as having 5 prestige-3 tiles, you may refresh it once by setting aside all market tiles, drawing new ones, sorting them, and returning the set-aside tiles to the bag. You are now ready to play.

walkthrough.title: Game Structure: Seasons and Rounds
walkthrough.text: Obsession is played over four seasons, each tracked by the white pawn on the round track. In standard play, there are 16 rounds total across the four seasons. Each season consists of several ordinary (non-courtship) rounds followed by one courtship round that ends the season. The ordinary rounds are marked by purple spaces on the track, and the courtship round is marked by a green space. You will play through all four seasons, ending at the Final Courtship.

walkthrough.title: Theme Cards
walkthrough.text: At the first round of each season, reveal a theme card from the theme deck. Theme cards show one of the five tile categories. Keep all revealed theme cards visible throughout the game, but make sure the most recent one is clearly distinguishable. The theme card determines which category of improvement tiles matters for that season's courtship scoring. Players with the highest total victory points in tiles of the current theme category win the courtship.

walkthrough.title: Ordinary Round Overview
walkthrough.text: In each ordinary round, players take turns clockwise starting from the first player. On your turn, you must either host an activity by performing a standard turn, or pass. You cannot do both. Once each player has taken one turn, the round ends and the white pawn advances to the next space on the round track.

walkthrough.title: Special Actions (Anytime During Your Turn)
walkthrough.text: Before we get into the standard turn, know that you have three special actions available anytime during your turn, whether you are hosting or passing. First, you may gain 100 coins at a cost of 2 reputation. You can repeat this as many times as you like. Second, you may refresh 1 servant, moving them to Available Service, at a cost of 3 reputation. Also repeatable. Third, you may refresh the Builders' Market at a cost of 4 reputation, but only once per turn. Important: a special action that would drop your reputation level below 1 is not allowed.

walkthrough.title: Standard Turn: Start of Turn Housekeeping
walkthrough.text: When you take a standard turn, you first handle housekeeping. Move servants in your Servants' Quarters area to Available Service. Then move servants in Expended Service to Servants' Quarters. This means servants take two full rounds to cycle back to being available — one round resting in Servants' Quarters, then one round moving to Available. If it is a Village Fair round and you have flipped your Private Study, receive 2 reputation and 300 coins. If it is an Objective Card round, draw 2 objective cards. Then gain 1 reputation for each Monument tile on display in your tableau. Finally, if you own Servants' Hall, you may deploy a servant to the hall and choose another player to gossip about — that player loses 1 reputation, you gain 1, and the servant moves to Expended Service.

walkthrough.title: Standard Turn Step 1: Host Activity
walkthrough.text: To perform a standard turn, you must host an activity. Choose one improvement tile from your tableau that has an activity listed in white text near the top left. Move that tile from your tableau to your Activity space on your player board. Your reputation level must be equal to or greater than the prestige of that tile, which is the white number in the wreath at the bottom left. There is an exception during National Holiday rounds, when prestige requirements are waived.

walkthrough.title: Standard Turn Step 2: Invite Guests
walkthrough.text: Play gentry cards from your active hand that match the types the tile requires. All indicated guest types must be played. Your reputation level must equal or exceed the prestige number of each guest card, shown at the top left. Family members and Fairchild cards are exceptions — they never have prestige requirements. Again, during National Holiday rounds, prestige requirements for guests are waived.

walkthrough.title: Standard Turn Step 3: Provide Service
walkthrough.text: Move the required servants from Available Service to the tile and the gentry cards. The types of servants needed are indicated on each tile and card. Cards with a gray meeple icon do not require servants. If you have the Servants' Quarters tile, one servant may come from Servants' Quarters instead of Available Service. When no Lady's Maid is available, a Housekeeper may substitute. For a Butler, Valet, or Footman, an Underbutler may substitute. When no Valet is available and you have the Brushing Room, a Footman may substitute.

walkthrough.title: Standard Turn Step 4: Enjoy Favors
walkthrough.text: Now collect the favors from your hosted activity. Favors are mandatory except for dismissing a guest. The types of favors include: Money — gain the coins indicated, though some guests called paupers cost you money. Reputation — gain 1 reputation for each lion rampant icon. Your young lady also gains reputation when hosted with a prestige male guest. Guests — for each 1-fleur-de-lis icon, draw a casual guest; for each 2-fleur-de-lis icon, draw a prestige guest. Your elder lady may draw 2 casual guests and return 1 to the bottom of its deck, or dismiss 1 guest instead. VP Card — draw a victory point card to hold for end-game points or play anytime for its benefit. Gossip — decrease another player's reputation. Hire — with Butler's Room, take 2 servants from Servants for Hire. Dismiss Guests — optional; send any guest to the bottom of its supply deck.

walkthrough.title: Standard Turn Step 5: Buy from the Builders' Market
walkthrough.text: After enjoying favors, you may buy one tile from the Builders' Market or a tile reserve for its market price plus any cost modifier shown at the top right of the tile. You cannot buy a copy of a tile you already own. Place the purchased tile in its proper column in your tableau, front side up. If you buy a Monument or Servants' Hall, take a reminder token. If you buy a Butler's Pantry, gain an Underbutler and place it in Expended Service. During a Builders' Holiday round, you may buy as many tiles as you can afford. After purchasing, slide remaining market tiles to the left and draw replacements from the bag, placed front side up. Stack duplicates and sort only the new tiles by their sorting numbers.

walkthrough.title: Standard Turn Step 6: Clear Player Board
walkthrough.text: After buying, clear your player board. Move all servants from the activity tile and guest cards to Expended Service. Return the activity tile to your tableau. If it is a black rose tile, flip it to rose side up. If it has a white flower, flip it — hybrid tiles change category each time they are used, so keep them in their current column. Put the gentry cards that attended the event into your face-up discard pile.

walkthrough.title: Passing Your Turn
walkthrough.text: If you cannot or choose not to host an activity, you pass. When you pass, move all your servants to Available Service — this is a powerful reset. Handle Village Fair, Objective Card round, and Monument bonuses the same as a standard turn. Take your entire discard pile back into your active hand. Then either gain 200 coins or refresh the Builders' Market for free. You may still buy from the Builders' Market after passing, just like in a standard turn.

walkthrough.title: Courtship Rounds
walkthrough.text: At the end of each season, there is a courtship round. If a player holds a Fairchild card from a previous courtship, they return it to the table. In seasons 1 through 3, the player with the highest total VP in tiles of the current theme category wins. A sole winner adds their choice of Fairchild guest card to their active hand for the coming season. If there is a tie, nobody takes a Fairchild. Each winner also draws 1 VP card. In the final courtship (season 4), VP are added from tiles in each of the 4 theme categories, with repeated themes counting again. A sole winner takes a Fairchild card for its 8 VP. If tied, the player with the highest reputation takes the Fairchild. If still tied, nobody gets it.

walkthrough.title: Between Seasons
walkthrough.text: After each courtship, each player discards 1 objective card, and the discards are shuffled and added to the bottom of the objective deck. After each of the first 3 courtships, pass the first-player pawn counterclockwise. After the first courtship, open the Service Tile Reserve by moving all Service tiles from the Builders' Market to the reserve. After the second courtship, open the PR1 Tile Reserve by moving all prestige-1 tiles from the market to the reserve. After opening a reserve, refill the Builders' Market as usual.

walkthrough.title: Tile Anatomy
walkthrough.text: Understanding improvement tiles is key. The top left shows the tile's title in black, with its hosted activity in white text underneath. The top right may show a cost modifier, a building icon (start tile), a black rose, or a white flower (hybrid tile). Black rose tiles flip once when first used. Hybrid tiles with a white flower flip each time they are used, changing their category. The center shows either a benefit or the guest and servant requirements on the left side and favors granted on the right. The bottom left shows the prestige number in a white wreath. The bottom center shows the category, which determines which column the tile sits in on your tableau. The bottom right shows VP and the tile sorting number.

walkthrough.title: Servant Management
walkthrough.text: Servants cycle through three states. Available Service means they are ready to use. When you host an activity and assign servants, they move to Expended Service at the end of your turn. Next turn, they move from Expended Service to Servants' Quarters. The turn after that, they move from Servants' Quarters back to Available Service. This two-round cooldown means managing your servants carefully is critical. The five servant types are Butler, Housekeeper, Valet, Lady's Maid, and Footman. Underbutlers can substitute for Butler, Valet, or Footman. When no Lady's Maid is available, a Housekeeper can substitute.

walkthrough.title: Reputation System
walkthrough.text: Reputation is tracked on your player board's reputation wheel using a main central counter and an incremental marker that orbits it. Reputation for scoring, player rank, and prestige thresholds is based solely on the main level — the increment is irrelevant for these purposes. Reputation is gained or lost in small increments. When the orbiting marker completes a rotation from 5 back to 1, you increment the central counter to the next value. In standard play, reputation increments from 6 to Max. Reputation can never drop below level 1, increment 1. At Max, when the outer marker would pass 5, it stays at 5.

walkthrough.title: Game End and Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: After the Final Courtship, move the white pawn to the wedding bells icon and tally scores across seven categories. First, VP on tiles — the white numbers in wreaths at bottom right of each tile. Second, VP on gentry cards — the number at top right of each card in your collection. Third, VP for each completed objective card. Fourth, VP for reputation based on a scoring table: level 1 earns 1 VP, level 2 earns 3, level 3 earns 6, level 4 earns 10, level 5 earns 15, level 6 earns 21, and Max earns 28 in standard play. Fifth, 2 VP for each servant you have, regardless of type. Sixth, 1 VP for each 200 coins, rounded down. Seventh, VP from retained victory point cards. Tiebreaker: the holder of a Fairchild card wins. If still tied, highest reputation wins. If still tied, the victory is shared.

bullets: 4 seasons, each with ordinary rounds + 1 courtship round

White pawn tracks progress on the round track

walkthrough.title: Welcome & Meet the Players
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first season with three example players. Three players is the recommended count for Obsession. Each player has chosen a different family and has their player board set up with 5 start tiles, 5 servants in Available Service, 4 family member cards, and 2 start guests. Let's call them Player 1, Player 2, and Player 3. Follow along even if you have a different number of players at your table.

walkthrough.title: Season 1 Begins: Reveal the Theme Card
walkthrough.text: At the start of the first round, reveal a theme card from the deck. Let's say it shows the Sporting category. This means that at the courtship at the end of this season, the player with the most VP in Sporting tiles will win and claim a Fairchild guest card. Keep this in mind as you plan your purchases from the Builders' Market this season.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Turn: Hosting an Activity
walkthrough.text: Player 1 decides to host an activity using Bowling Green, one of their start tiles. Bowling Green is a Sporting tile with prestige 1. Player 1 moves it from their tableau to the Activity space. The tile requires certain guest types, so Player 1 plays their son and one of their start guests from their active hand. Then Player 1 assigns servants — the tile and cards indicate they need a Footman and a Valet, so those two move from Available Service to the tile and cards.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Collecting Favors and Buying
walkthrough.text: Player 1 now collects favors from Bowling Green. They gain some coins and a reputation increment from a lion rampant icon. Then they check the Builders' Market. They spot a tile in the Sporting category that costs 300. Player 1 buys it and places it in the Sporting column of their tableau. Smart move — they are already building toward the Sporting courtship this season. Finally, Player 1 clears their board: servants go to Expended Service, the tile returns to the tableau, and the gentry cards go to the discard pile.

walkthrough.title: Why That Was a Good Move
walkthrough.text: Player 1 accomplished three things in one turn. They hosted an activity to earn favors, they built reputation from the lion rampant icon, and they purchased a Sporting tile to prepare for the season's courtship. Buying tiles in the current theme category is one of the most important strategic decisions in the early game, because courtship victories earn you Fairchild cards and VP cards.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Turn: Hosting with Front Parlour
walkthrough.text: Player 2 hosts an activity using Front Parlour, a Prestige category start tile. They play their wife and a start guest, assigning a Lady's Maid and Housekeeper from Available Service. Front Parlour grants favors including drawing a casual guest from the 1-fleur-de-lis deck. Player 2 draws a new casual guest and adds them to their active hand. More guests means more flexibility in future turns.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Turn: Hosting with Main Gazebo
walkthrough.text: Player 3 chooses Main Gazebo. They play their daughter and a start guest, assign the required servants, and collect favors including some coins and a reputation increment. Player 3 scans the Builders' Market and finds a tile they want but it costs 500. They only have 200. Player 3 decides not to buy this turn and saves their money for later.

walkthrough.title: Round 2: Servant Cycling in Action
walkthrough.text: It is now round 2. At the start of each player's turn, they handle servant housekeeping. Player 1 moves any servants in Servants' Quarters to Available Service, and servants in Expended Service to Servants' Quarters. The two servants Player 1 used last round are now in Servants' Quarters — they will not be available again until round 3. Player 1 still has their Butler, Housekeeper, and Lady's Maid in Available Service, so they can still host an activity this round.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Mistake: Ignoring Servant Supply
walkthrough.text: Player 2 wants to host another activity, but they used their Lady's Maid and Housekeeper last round. Those servants are now in Expended Service and will only move to Servants' Quarters this turn. Player 2 has their Butler, Valet, and Footman available. They look at their tiles but cannot find one that matches these servant types with the guests they have available. Player 2 is stuck — they have to pass this turn.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Plan Your Servant Rotation
walkthrough.text: The two-round servant cooldown is one of the most important mechanics in Obsession. If you burn all your key servants in one turn, you may be forced to pass the next turn because you lack the right servant types. Before hosting, always check which servants you will need in the next round. Plan activities that use different servant types each turn to keep your options open.

walkthrough.title: Player 2 Passes — But It's Not All Bad
walkthrough.text: Player 2 passes their turn. Passing is not a complete waste. When you pass, all your servants move to Available Service immediately — a full reset. You also take your entire discard pile back into your active hand, so those guests from last round are playable again. And Player 2 gets to choose: gain 200 coins or refresh the Builders' Market for free. Player 2 takes the 200 coins. They can even still buy a tile from the market this turn.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Mistake: Spending Reputation Recklessly
walkthrough.text: Player 3 sees a tile they want in the Builders' Market but it is not quite right for their strategy. They use a special action to refresh the Builders' Market for 4 reputation, hoping for something better. The new tiles are not great either. Player 3 uses another special action to gain 100 coins, losing 2 more reputation. They have now spent 6 reputation in special actions and their reputation level has dropped. This will restrict which tiles and guests they can use next turn.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Reputation Is Your Most Precious Resource
walkthrough.text: Reputation unlocks everything in Obsession. Higher reputation lets you host better tiles, invite higher-prestige guests, and scores heavily at game end — level 6 is worth 21 VP in standard play. Spending reputation on special actions feels tempting in the moment, but the long-term cost is enormous. Every reputation point spent on 100 coins is reputation you are not building toward higher prestige thresholds. Use special actions sparingly and only when the payoff clearly outweighs the reputation loss.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Mistake: Ignoring the Theme
walkthrough.text: Through the first three rounds, Player 3 has been buying tiles from various categories but none in Sporting, the current theme. The courtship is coming and Player 3 has zero VP in the Sporting category. Player 1, by contrast, has their Bowling Green start tile plus the Sporting tile they bought in round 1. Player 3 will lose the courtship badly.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Build Toward the Theme
walkthrough.text: Each season's courtship rewards the player with the most VP in the current theme category's tiles. Courtship victories give you a Fairchild card, which is an 8 VP guest with no prestige requirement, and a VP card for additional points. Ignoring the theme means losing out on two scoring opportunities. Always check the revealed theme card and try to buy at least one or two tiles in that category each season.

walkthrough.title: More Rounds, Then Courtship
walkthrough.text: Play continues for the remaining ordinary rounds of season 1. Each round, players take one turn — hosting an activity or passing. Servants cycle through their three-state rotation. Players buy tiles, draw guests, gain reputation, and maneuver for the courtship. When the white pawn reaches the green courtship space, the season's ordinary play is over.

walkthrough.title: The First Courtship
walkthrough.text: It is courtship time. Each player totals the VP on their Sporting tiles. Player 1 has the most Sporting VP and wins the courtship as the sole winner. Player 1 chooses one of the two Fairchild guest cards — either Charles or Elizabeth Fairchild — and adds it to their active hand. Player 1 also draws a VP card. Each player then discards 1 objective card. The first-player pawn passes counterclockwise. After this first courtship, all Service tiles in the Builders' Market move to the Service Tile Reserve, and the market is refilled from the bag.

walkthrough.title: Continue Playing the Remaining Seasons
walkthrough.text: Season 2 begins with a new theme card revealed. Players continue hosting activities, managing servants, buying tiles, and building reputation. Remember the key lessons from this practice season: plan your servant rotation to avoid forced passes, protect your reputation from wasteful special actions, and always build toward the current theme category for courtship scoring. By season 3 and 4, higher-prestige tiles and guests become available as your reputation grows.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have walked through a complete first season. You have seen how to host activities, cycle servants, buy tiles, and compete in a courtship. You watched Player 2 learn about servant management the hard way, and Player 3 learn that reputation and theme focus are critical. You are ready to continue into the remaining seasons on your own.

walkthrough.title: Before You Begin Your Own Game
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics and common mistakes to avoid.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to tile types, servant rules, guest types, and game terms.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

bullets: Scripted tutorial with 3 example players (recommended player count)
Each has a family board, 5 start tiles, 5 servants, family cards + 2 start guests
Follow along even with a different player count

Cannot match available servants to any valid tile + guest combination

summary.title: Lesson: Plan Your Servant Rotation
bullets: Two-round cooldown is a core mechanic — plan around it
Use different servant types each turn to stay flexible
Always check next round's needs before committing servants

summary.title: Player 2 Passes — But It's Not All Bad

Can still buy tiles from the market after passing

summary.title: Player 3's Mistake: Spending Reputation Recklessly
bullets: Spent 4 reputation to refresh market, 2 more for 100 coins = 6 total
Reputation level dropped — restricts future tile and guest access
Short-term gains, long-term damage

summary.title: Lesson: Reputation Is Your Most Precious Resource
bullets: Reputation unlocks better tiles and higher-prestige guests
Reputation VP scale is exponential: level 6 = 21 VP in standard play
Use special actions sparingly — the reputation cost adds up fast

summary.title: Player 3's Mistake: Ignoring the Theme
bullets: No Sporting tiles purchased despite Sporting being the theme
Will lose courtship to Player 1 who focused on Sporting
Missing out on Fairchild card and VP card

summary.title: Lesson: Build Toward the Theme
bullets: Courtship rewards: Fairchild card (8 VP guest) + VP card
Buy 1-2 tiles in the theme category each season
Ignoring the theme means losing two scoring opportunities

summary.title: More Rounds, Then Courtship
bullets: Continue ordinary rounds until white pawn reaches courtship (green) space
Each round: host or pass, manage servants, buy tiles
Build reputation and prepare for courtship scoring

summary.title: The First Courtship
bullets: Compare VP in Sporting tiles — sole winner gets Fairchild card + VP card
Ties = no Fairchild awarded
Discard 1 objective card; pass first-player pawn counterclockwise
After courtship 1: Service tiles move to reserve, refill market

summary.title: Continue Playing the Remaining Seasons

Higher-prestige tiles and guests become accessible as reputation grows

Saw Player 3 learn about reputation and theme importance
Ready to play the remaining seasons on your own

summary.title: Before You Begin Your Own Game
bullets: Check out General Tips for strategy basics for beginners
Reference the Appendix for tile types, servants, and game terms
Ask your personal GM Guide — tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before diving into strategy, here are the biggest mistakes new players make in Obsession. First, burning reputation on special actions early in the game — that reputation is far more valuable for unlocking high-prestige tiles and guests, and it scores heavily at game end. Second, not planning servant rotation and getting forced to pass when you wanted to host. Third, ignoring the theme card and losing courtships that could have given you a Fairchild card worth 8 VP plus a VP card. Fourth, hoarding money instead of buying tiles — tiles are your primary source of VP and they power your ability to host better activities. Keep these in mind and you will have a much smoother game.

walkthrough.title: Servant Rotation Strategy
walkthrough.text: The two-round servant cooldown is the heartbeat of Obsession. After you use a servant, it goes to Expended Service, then to Servants' Quarters, then finally back to Available Service. This means you need to plan at least two turns ahead. Try to alternate which servant types you use each turn. If you use your Valet and Footman this turn, make sure you have your Butler and Lady's Maid available for next turn. If you find yourself consistently running out of servants, consider investing in hiring more through Butler's Room or buying the Butler's Pantry tile to gain an Underbutler.

walkthrough.title: When to Pass Strategically
walkthrough.text: Passing is not a failure — it is a powerful tool when used at the right time. When you pass, all your servants instantly reset to Available Service, and your entire discard pile returns to your active hand. This is especially valuable when your best guests are in your discard pile and your servants are scattered across Expended Service and Servants' Quarters. You also get to either gain 200 coins or refresh the Builders' Market for free, and you can still buy a tile. Plan a pass every 2-3 rounds to keep your engine running smoothly.

walkthrough.title: Building Reputation Efficiently
walkthrough.text: Reputation is the most important resource in Obsession. It gates which tiles you can host, which guests you can invite, and it scores exponentially at game end. At level 1 you get 1 VP, but at level 6 you get 21 VP — a massive swing. The most efficient way to build reputation is through activities that grant lion rampant icons. Your young lady also gains bonus reputation whenever she attends an activity with a prestige male guest. Focus on activities that provide reputation as a favor, and avoid special actions that burn reputation for coins unless absolutely necessary.

walkthrough.title: Tile Buying Strategy
walkthrough.text: Tiles are your estate and your primary source of VP. When buying from the Builders' Market, consider three things. First, does the tile match the current theme category? Courtship scoring is based on tile VP in the theme category. Second, does the tile provide good favors for its cost? Look at what guests and servants it requires versus what it gives back. Third, does the tile's prestige match your current reputation level? Buying a prestige 4 tile when you are at reputation level 2 means you cannot use it until your reputation catches up. Balance your purchases between affordable tiles you can use now and aspirational tiles that will pay off later.

walkthrough.title: Guest Management
walkthrough.text: Your active hand of gentry cards determines what activities you can host each turn. Family members are always available, but start guests and acquired guests cycle through your active hand and discard pile. Drawing new guests through activity favors expands your options. Casual guests from the 1-fleur-de-lis deck are lower prestige but easier to use. Prestige guests from the 2-fleur-de-lis deck have higher prestige requirements but are worth more VP and often provide better favors. Do not neglect the dismiss guest favor — removing low-value or costly pauper guests from your collection keeps your hand lean and efficient.

walkthrough.title: Objective Card Management
walkthrough.text: You start with 5 objective cards in standard play and must discard 1 after each courtship. This means you will complete the game with only a few objectives. Look at your objectives early and identify which ones naturally align with your strategy. If an objective rewards having many servants, prioritize hiring. If it rewards tiles in a specific category, buy those tiles. You draw 2 additional objective cards during Objective Card rounds, so your objectives evolve over time. Discard objectives that do not fit your path — do not try to score all of them.

walkthrough.title: Timing Your Engine Across Seasons
walkthrough.text: Obsession has a natural arc across its four seasons. In season 1, focus on building your foundation — use your start tiles, gain a few guests, and buy tiles in the first theme category. In season 2, begin expanding your servant pool and start hosting more ambitious activities as your reputation rises. Season 3 is the pivot point where high-prestige tiles and guests become accessible. Season 4 is the scoring sprint — maximize tile VP, complete objectives, and push your reputation as high as possible before final scoring. The final courtship counts tiles from all four theme categories, so diverse tile collections are rewarded.

Plan servant rotation to avoid forced passes
Don't ignore the theme card — courtship victories are worth 8+ VP
Buy tiles instead of hoarding money — tiles are your primary VP source

Hire more servants via Butler's Room or buy Butler's Pantry for an Underbutler

Gain 200 coins or free market refresh when passing
Can still buy tiles after passing
Plan a strategic pass every 2-3 rounds

summary.title: Building Reputation Efficiently
bullets: Reputation VP is exponential: level 1 = 1 VP, level 6 = 21 VP
Host activities with lion rampant icons for reputation gains
Young lady gains bonus reputation with prestige male guests
Avoid special actions that trade reputation for coins

summary.title: Tile Buying Strategy
bullets: Match the current theme category for courtship scoring
Evaluate favor output vs. guest/servant cost
Only buy tiles whose prestige you can currently meet (or will soon)
Balance usable-now tiles with aspirational purchases

summary.title: Guest Management
bullets: Family members always available; other guests cycle through discard
Casual guests (1 fleur-de-lis): lower prestige, easier to use
Prestige guests (2 fleur-de-lis): higher prestige, more VP, better favors
Use dismiss to remove pauper guests and keep hand lean

summary.title: Objective Card Management
bullets: Start with 5 objectives, discard 1 each courtship — focus on 2-3 key ones
Draw 2 more during Objective Card rounds
Align objectives with your natural strategy
Discard objectives that don't fit rather than stretching too thin

summary.title: Timing Your Engine Across Seasons
bullets: Season 1: foundation — start tiles, early guests, first theme tiles
Season 2: expand servant pool, host more ambitious activities
Season 3: pivot — high-prestige tiles and guests now accessible
Season 4: scoring sprint — maximize tile VP, objectives, reputation

walkthrough.title: Reputation Racing Strategy
walkthrough.text: One of the strongest approaches in Obsession is racing your reputation as fast as possible. High reputation unlocks prestige 4, 5, and 6 tiles and guests, which are worth far more VP and provide far better favors. The key is to host activities with lion rampant icons every turn and pair your young lady with prestige male guests for bonus reputation. Each lion rampant gives 1 reputation increment, and those increments compound quickly. By season 2, if you have reached reputation level 3 or 4, you gain access to tiles and guests that slower players simply cannot use yet.

walkthrough.title: Servant Economy Mastery
walkthrough.text: Advanced players treat servants as a scarce currency. Each servant in your pool is worth 2 VP at game end, and having more servants available means you can host activities more often without being forced to pass. The Butler's Room is your hiring engine — when you host with it, you may take 2 servants from Servants For Hire. When flipped, Butler's Room lets you recruit 1 servant from another player's board, which is both a gain for you and a denial for them. Butler's Pantry grants an Underbutler, who can substitute for Butler, Valet, or Footman. Building your servant pool early means more hosting, more favors, and more VP from servants at game end.

walkthrough.title: Courtship Dominance
walkthrough.text: Winning courtships consistently is a major point accelerator. Each courtship victory gives you a Fairchild guest card worth 8 VP at game end, plus a VP card for additional points. In seasons 1-3, the winner is determined by total VP in tiles of the current theme category. Plan your tile purchases around the revealed theme card. If the theme is Sporting, aggressively buy Sporting tiles. In the final courtship, VP from all four theme categories are totaled, with repeated themes counting again. This means a player who has invested broadly across categories can score well, but a player who focused on themes that appeared multiple times gets a huge boost.

walkthrough.title: Monument Strategy
walkthrough.text: Monuments are special tiles that provide 1 reputation increment at the start of every turn you take, whether hosting or passing. This passive reputation gain is extraordinarily powerful over the course of a game. The Sculpture Garden is the most coveted Monument. When you have two or three Monuments, you gain 2-3 reputation every single round without spending any actions on it. Monuments are only available in limited quantities based on player count, so acquiring them early is a significant advantage. The reminder token on your board ensures you never forget your Monument bonus.

walkthrough.title: Hybrid Tile Strategy
walkthrough.text: Hybrid tiles, identified by a white flower icon, flip each time they are used, changing their category. This makes them uniquely flexible. A hybrid tile can contribute VP to one category for one courtship, then flip and contribute to a different category for the next courtship. Experienced players time their hybrid tile usage to align the tile's current category with the upcoming courtship theme. Remember that when you flip a hybrid tile, the background color of the servant icon shows the reverse side's color, helping you plan ahead. Keeping hybrid tiles in their current column ensures your tableau stays organized.

walkthrough.title: Black Rose Tile Timing
walkthrough.text: Black rose tiles start with a black rose visible on one side. The other side, the front, is the stronger version. The tile flips once when first used, revealing the front side permanently. This means the first time you host with a black rose tile, you are playing the weaker version, but every subsequent use is the improved version. Smart players time their first use of a black rose tile when they need the activity but do not mind weaker favors, knowing that future uses will be more powerful. Do not delay using black rose tiles just because the first use is weaker — getting them flipped early means more uses of the stronger side.

walkthrough.title: Guest Prestige Ladder
walkthrough.text: Guests in Obsession form a ladder of increasing prestige and value. Start guests have prestige 1 and 0 VP. Casual guests range from prestige 1-2 with variable VP, some even negative (paupers with -1 to -4 VP). Prestige guests have prestige 3-6 and are worth 2-6 VP each. The key insight is that higher-prestige guests not only score more VP themselves but unlock better activities and provide stronger favors. As your reputation rises, strategically replace low-value guests by using the dismiss favor. Curate your guest collection like a portfolio — aim for high-prestige, high-VP guests while removing paupers that drag your score down.

walkthrough.title: Servants' Hall and Gossip Attacks
walkthrough.text: Servants' Hall is a unique tile that does not move to your Activity space. Instead, at the start of each turn, you may deploy a servant to the hall to gossip about another player. The targeted player loses 1 reputation, and you gain 1. This is a 2-reputation swing each round. Over a full game, that adds up to a massive reputation advantage. The cost is losing a servant to Expended Service each turn, which makes servant management even more critical. Servants' Hall is most effective when you have a large servant pool to support the ongoing cost.

walkthrough.title: VP Card Optimization
walkthrough.text: Victory point cards can be held for end-game scoring or played anytime for their immediate benefit. Advanced players evaluate each VP card by asking: is the immediate benefit worth more than the end-game VP? Sometimes playing a VP card to gain reputation or money at a critical moment is more valuable than the 2-4 VP it would score at game end, especially if the immediate benefit enables a courtship victory or a key tile purchase. Keep VP cards flexible and play them when the timing is right.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Scoring Optimization
walkthrough.text: In the final scoring, there are seven categories: tile VP, gentry card VP, objective card VP, reputation VP, 2 VP per servant, 1 VP per 200 coins, and VP cards. Advanced players do not neglect any category. Servants at 2 VP each add up fast — a player with 8 servants scores 16 VP from servants alone. The reputation table is exponential, so the difference between level 4 (10 VP) and level 6 (21 VP) is 11 points. Coins at 1 VP per 200 are less efficient but still matter. Balance your investments across all scoring categories rather than over-investing in one.

Host activities with lion rampant icons every turn

Reaching level 3-4 by season 2 gives a major advantage

Butler's Pantry grants an Underbutler (substitutes for Butler/Valet/Footman)
Build servant pool early for compounding benefit

summary.title: Courtship Dominance

Buy tiles in the current theme category aggressively

Consistent courtship wins can swing 20+ VP over the game

summary.title: Endgame Scoring Optimization
bullets: 7 scoring categories: tiles, gentry, objectives, reputation, servants, coins, VP cards
Servants: 2 VP each — 8 servants = 16 VP
Reputation: exponential — level 4 = 10 VP, level 6 = 21 VP
Don't neglect any category; balance investments across all seven

entries.definition: The main counter on your player board's reputation wheel. Only the main level matters for prestige thresholds, player rank, and scoring. The incremental marker orbiting it tracks progress toward the next level. Standard play ranges from 1 to Max (6 levels + Max).

A small gain or loss of reputation tracked by the black hexagonal prism orbiting the main counter on the reputation wheel. Completing a full orbit (5 to 1) advances the main level. Written in the rules as 'y' notation.
The white number in a wreath at the bottom left of an improvement tile. Your reputation level must equal or exceed a tile's prestige to host an activity with it. Tiles range from prestige 1 to 6.
The number at the top left of a guest card. Your reputation level must equal or exceed a guest's prestige to invite them. Family members and Fairchild cards are exempt from this requirement.

The gentry cards (family members + guests) currently available for you to play on your turn. When you host an activity, used guests go to your discard pile. When you pass, your entire discard pile returns to your active hand.
Face-up pile where gentry cards go after being used in an activity. Cards remain here until you pass, at which point they all return to your active hand. Players may examine their own discard piles.
A turn in which you host an activity. Involves housekeeping, hosting a tile, inviting guests, providing service, enjoying favors, buying tiles, and clearing your board. The alternative is to pass.

Choosing not to host an activity. Resets all servants to Available Service, returns discard pile to active hand. Gain 200 coins or free market refresh. You may still buy tiles. A strategic tool, not a failure.

Benefits gained from hosting an activity. Include money, reputation (lion rampant icons), drawing guests (fleur-de-lis icons), VP cards, gossip attacks, hiring servants, and dismissing guests. All favors are mandatory except dismissal.
A heraldic icon showing a lion standing on one hind foot with forefeet in the air. Each lion rampant icon on a tile or card grants 1 reputation increment as a favor.
The area on your player board where servants ready to be assigned to activities are placed. Servants start here and return here after cycling through Expended Service and Servants' Quarters.
The area where servants go after being used in an activity (when clearing your board). At the start of your next turn, they move to Servants' Quarters.

The resting area for servants between Expended Service and Available Service. At the start of your turn, servants here move to Available Service. Servants spend one full round in Quarters before becoming available.
One of the five start servant types. A high-ranking male servant. Can be substituted by an Underbutler. Each player starts with 1 Butler.
One of the five start servant types. A high-ranking female servant who manages the household. Can substitute for a Lady's Maid when none is available. Each player starts with 1 Housekeeper.
One of the five start servant types. A personal servant for a gentleman. Can be substituted by an Underbutler. When no Valet is available and you own the Brushing Room tile, a Footman may substitute.
One of the five start servant types. A personal servant for a lady. When unavailable, a Housekeeper may substitute. Each player starts with 1 Lady's Maid.

One of the five start servant types. A general-purpose male servant. Can be substituted by an Underbutler. Can substitute for a Valet when you own the Brushing Room. Each player starts with 1 Footman (York starts with 2).
A special servant gained only when purchasing the Butler's Pantry tile. Starts in Expended Service. Can substitute for Butler, Valet, or Footman. Only 2 Underbutlers exist in the game.

A supply on the Derbyshire board containing Valets, Lady's Maids, and Footmen available for hiring. Stocked per player: 1 Valet, 1 Lady's Maid, 2 Footmen each. Servants are hired through the Butler's Room favor.

Gentry cards representing your family: husband, wife, son, and daughter. Always in your active hand or assigned to an activity. No prestige requirement to invite. Cannot be dismissed. Asquith has a 5th member (Maud).
One of 15 special guest cards dealt during setup (2 per player). Identified by a crown below the fleur-de-lis. Prestige 1 and 0 VP. Unused start guests are shuffled into the casual guest deck.
Drawn from the 1-fleur-de-lis deck. Prestige 1-2. VP range from -4 to 2. There are 35 casual guest cards plus unused start guests. Lower prestige requirements make them accessible early in the game.
Drawn from the 2-fleur-de-lis deck. Prestige 3-6. VP range from 2-6. There are 25 prestige guest cards. Require higher reputation to invite but provide better favors and more end-game VP.
A casual guest with negative VP (ranging from -1 to -4). Paupers cost you money when hosted. They count against your score at game end. Use the dismiss favor to remove them from your collection.

Two special guest cards (Charles and Elizabeth Fairchild) with a crest in a purple frame. Worth 8 VP each. Awarded to courtship winners. No prestige requirement. Returned to the table at the start of each courtship.

A female family member (typically the wife or dowager) who has a special ability when drawing casual guests: she may draw 2 casual guests and return 1 to the bottom of the deck, or dismiss 1 guest instead.
A female family member (typically the daughter) who gains bonus reputation when she attends an activity with a prestige male guest, including Charles Fairchild.

A tile representing a room or space on your estate. Placed in your tableau by category. Used to host activities. Has a prestige level, VP value, category, and sorting number. The main building blocks of your estate.
An improvement tile with a black rose on one side. The non-rose side is the front (stronger) version. The tile flips once when first used, permanently revealing the front side for all future uses.

An improvement tile with a white flower icon. The side with the underlined title is the front. This tile flips each time it is used, changing its category. The servant icon background shows the reverse side's color.

A special tile type that provides 1 reputation increment at the start of every turn (hosting or passing). Limited quantity based on player count. Does not show category in text — match by background color. Includes Sculpture Garden.

The most powerful Monument tile. Added to the tile bag during setup. Provides passive reputation gain each turn. Can optionally be excluded from the game or substituted with Garden Maze if players agree.
One of the 5 start improvement tiles. When flipped, during Village Fair rounds it provides 2 reputation and 300 coins as a bonus at the start of your turn, whether hosting or passing.

One of the 5 start improvement tiles. Provides the Hire favor: take 2 servants (Valet, Lady's Maid, or Footman) from Servants For Hire. When flipped, you may instead recruit 1 of those types from another player's board.
One of the 5 start improvement tiles given to each player during setup. A basic activity tile for hosting guests in your estate grounds.
One of the 5 start improvement tiles. A Prestige category tile used for hosting indoor social activities with guests.
One of the 5 start improvement tiles. A Sporting category tile used for outdoor leisure activities. Useful for building toward Sporting courtship scoring.

A purchasable improvement tile (included in initial market setup). When owned, allows one servant per activity to come from Servants' Quarters instead of Available Service, improving servant flexibility.

A purchasable tile that enables gossip attacks. At the start of each turn, you may deploy a servant to gossip — target player loses 1 reputation, you gain 1. The servant goes to Expended Service. Does not move to Activity space.
A purchasable tile included in the initial market. Upon purchasing, gain 1 Underbutler placed in Expended Service. Underbutlers substitute for Butler, Valet, or Footman.
A purchasable tile included in the initial market. When owned, allows a Footman to substitute for a Valet when no Valet is available.
A purchasable tile that can be included in the initial Builders' Market during setup. One of the Service category tiles.

A special tile with a unique mechanic. When used, draw 1 prestige guest from the supply to stay in the suite. The guest's servant requirements are ignored — the suite's Butler handles all needs. The guest provides doubled favors.
A tile that, when flipped, allows dismissing any number of guests in a single turn. Useful for mass-clearing paupers or unwanted guests from your collection.

The row of tiles available for purchase on the Derbyshire board. Tiles are sorted by sorting number, ascending left to right. After purchases, tiles slide left and replacements are drawn from the bag.

Separate areas where certain tile types move after courtships. Service Tile Reserve opens after courtship 1. PR1 (prestige 1) Tile Reserve opens after courtship 2. Tiles in reserves can still be purchased.

Setting aside all market tiles, drawing new ones from the bag front side up, stacking duplicates, sorting by sorting number, and returning set-aside tiles to the bag. Costs 4 reputation as a special action, or is free when passing.
A number shown at the top right of some improvement tiles that adjusts the purchase price. Added to the tile's base market price when buying from the Builders' Market.
The small black number at the bottom right of each improvement tile, just up and left of the VP value. Used to sort tiles in the Builders' Market in ascending order from left to right.

The round that ends each season (green space on the round track). Players compare VP in tiles of the current theme category. The sole winner gains a Fairchild guest card and draws a VP card. Ties result in no Fairchild being awarded (except in the final courtship).

One of 10 cards showing a tile category. Revealed at the first round of each season. Determines which tile category counts for that season's courtship. All revealed themes remain visible throughout the game.

The last courtship at the end of season 4. VP are totaled from all four theme categories, with repeated themes counting again. Ties are broken by reputation. The sole winner takes a Fairchild card for its 8 VP.
A non-courtship round (purple space on the round track). Players take one turn each in clockwise order — host an activity or pass. Most rounds in the game are ordinary rounds.
A special ordinary round type. If you have flipped your Private Study, you receive 2 reputation and 300 coins at the start of your turn, whether hosting or passing.
A special ordinary round type. At the start of your turn, draw 2 objective cards from the supply, whether hosting or passing.
A special ordinary round type where prestige requirements for tiles and guests are waived. Players can host any tile and invite any guest regardless of reputation level.
A special ordinary round type where you may buy as many tiles as you can afford from the Builders' Market, instead of the usual limit of one tile per turn.

Secret scoring goals dealt during setup (5 per player in standard, 4 in extended). Award VP at game end if conditions are met. Each player discards 1 after each courtship. Additional objectives drawn during Objective Card rounds.
One of 30 cards drawn as favors or courtship rewards. Can be held for end-game VP or played anytime for an immediate benefit. Played VP cards return to the bottom of the deck.
Victory points shown on improvement tiles — the white number in a wreath at the bottom right. Counted in courtship comparisons and scored at game end.
Victory points shown on gentry cards at the top right. Family members, start guests, casual guests, prestige guests, and Fairchild cards all have VP values counted at game end.

End-game scoring for reputation level. Standard play: Level 1=1, 2=3, 3=6, 4=10, 5=15, 6=21, Max=28. Extended play adds: 7=28, 8=36, Max=45. Exponential growth makes high reputation extremely valuable.
At game end, each servant you possess is worth 2 VP regardless of type. This includes Butlers, Housekeepers, Valets, Lady's Maids, Footmen, and Underbutlers in any service area.
At game end, score 1 VP for each 200 coins you have, rounded down. Coins are silver (100) and gold (500).
Anytime during your turn, gain 100 coins at a cost of 2 reputation. May be repeated indefinitely. Cannot be used if it would drop reputation level below 1.
Anytime during your turn, move 1 servant to Available Service at a cost of 3 reputation. May be repeated indefinitely. Cannot be used if it would drop reputation level below 1.
Anytime during your turn, refresh the Builders' Market at a cost of 4 reputation. Limited to once per turn. Should only be used for your own benefit, not to deny options to other players.

An optional favor indicated by a fleur-de-lis with a trash icon. Allows you to remove any guest from anywhere in your cards (active hand or discard). The dismissed guest goes to the bottom of its supply deck. Family members cannot be dismissed. Dismissal occurs when clearing your board.

A favor type representing a social attack. Allows you to decrease the reputation of another player you choose. Some gossip attacks also cost you reputation. Available through certain tiles and through Servants' Hall.

A favor available through the Butler's Room tile. Take 2 servants (Valet, Lady's Maid, or Footman) from the Servants For Hire supply. With flipped Butler's Room, recruit 1 of those types from another player's board instead.

Your personal display of improvement tiles arranged in category columns under your player board. Tiles in your tableau are available for hosting activities and are scored at game end for VP. Also used for courtship comparisons.

The area on your player board where you place the improvement tile you are currently hosting an activity on. The tile, invited guests, and assigned servants are all placed here during a standard turn.

The classification shown at the bottom center of each improvement tile, determining which column it occupies in your tableau. Categories include Sporting, Prestige, Service, and others. Monuments are identified by background color rather than text.

The track on the Alderley Hall board that the white pawn advances along each round. Shows 16 spaces for standard play or 20 for extended play. Purple spaces are ordinary rounds, green spaces are courtship rounds.
The purple pawn given to the starting player. Play proceeds clockwise from the holder. Passes counterclockwise after each of the first 3 courtship rounds.
The central game board with the round track, VP card deck space, and theme card deck space. Has two sides: 16-round (standard play) and 20-round (extended play).
The second shared game board holding the Builders' Market, casual and prestige guest deck spaces, Servants For Hire area, objective card supply, and money supply.
Used to hold all improvement tiles (except Monuments and unused start tiles) during setup. Tiles are drawn from this bag to fill the Builders' Market. Monument tiles are added based on player count.

The circular track at the top right of each player board. Contains a central main counter and an orbiting hexagonal marker. The main counter shows your reputation level; the marker tracks incremental progress.

A token placed on your player board when you buy a Monument or Servants' Hall. Reminds you to take the passive reputation gain (Monument) or gossip option (Servants' Hall) at the start of each future turn.
One of the four player families. Unique trait: starts with their reputation marker on 4 instead of 1, giving early access to higher-prestige tiles and guests.
One of the four player families. Unique trait: starts with 1 additional Footman (6 servants total instead of 5), giving more hosting flexibility.
One of the four player families. Unique trait: includes a 5th family member, the dowager countess Maud, providing an extra card in the active hand.
One of the four player families. Unique trait: starts with 300 coins, giving an early economic advantage for tile purchases.
A game variant where the theme card is revealed during the courtship round instead of at the beginning of the season. Makes courtship preparation more uncertain.
A game variant where the Builders' Market (not the reserves) is refreshed after each of the first three courtships, adding more market variety between seasons.
A game variant where the leftmost tile (or stack) in the Builders' Market is removed after each round, then the market shifts and refills. Creates more urgency in purchasing decisions.
A game variant where at each season start, a random ordinary round is selected to reveal the theme card instead of always revealing it in the first round.
A novelty prestige-8 guest card (the developer's cat). Not intended for standard play. Playable only via chance draw into Lionheart Suite. Awards 9 VP at game end.
